cards.title: Apple overtakes Nvidia as the world's most valuable company — the first time the AI-chip king has lost the crown in nearly a year, as investors cool on the AI trade
cards.what_happened: On Friday, July 17, 2026, Apple overtook Nvidia to become the world's most valuable listed company, ending the day at about $4.88 trillion while Nvidia sat around $4.86 trillion after its shares fell 3.5%. It is the first time Apple has held the top spot since April 2025, and both firms are now circling the unprecedented $5 trillion mark. The shift wasn't driven by blockbuster Apple news — its shares were roughly flat — but by a sell-off in chipmakers: the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (a benchmark tracking major chip stocks) has dropped almost 19% from its all-time high this month as investors reassessed how sustainable the AI spending frenzy really is. Analysts framed the move as a 'rotation': money leaving the most capex-heavy AI names and flowing into Apple, seen as a safer way to profit from AI through its services, ecosystem and hardware upgrades.
key_points: Apple ends Friday worth ~$4.88 trillion, edging Nvidia's ~$4.86 trillion
Nvidia fell 3.5% as the AI-chip rally wobbled; Apple held steady
Apple's first time at No. 1 since April 2025 — both near the $5tn mark
The chip index is down almost 19% from its record high this month
cards.moot: The company at the very top of the market is really a snapshot of the story investors currently believe. In a boom, the crowd rushes to the most obvious winner — the one 'selling the shovels' — but when that story is questioned, money rotates to steadier earners, and the crown can change hands without the new leader doing anything new. Leadership is about the narrative as much as the numbers.

depth.background: The 'most valuable company' title is decided by market capitalisation — a company's share price multiplied by how many shares exist, i.e. what the whole business is worth on the stock market. For nearly a year that crown belonged to Nvidia, the chip designer whose processors ('AI accelerators') do the heavy computing behind the artificial-intelligence boom; as every big tech firm raced to buy its chips, Nvidia's value rocketed toward $5 trillion. Apple — the iPhone maker — had been the long-time leader before that but slipped as investors piled into the most obvious 'picks-and-shovels' winner of the AI gold rush. This week that trade wobbled: the same doubts that hit chip stocks after China's cheap Kimi K3 model launch knocked Nvidia and its peers, and money rotated toward Apple.
depth.why_it_matters: Which company sits at No. 1 is a barometer of what story the market believes. For a year, that story was 'whoever sells the AI hardware wins', and Nvidia embodied it. Apple retaking the crown without any big news of its own signals a shift in mood — investors are questioning whether the astronomical spending on AI chips will pay off, and are spreading their bets toward companies with steadier, proven earnings. When the single most crowded trade on Earth starts to unwind, it ripples through every index, pension fund and 401(k) that owns these giants.
depth.ripple_effects: A cooling AI-chip trade pressures not just Nvidia but the whole supply chain — AMD, Micron, TSMC, ASML and the AI 'story' stocks that soared on the boom. Because these mega-caps are such a huge slice of US indices like the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, a big swing in either name moves the entire market and the retirement savings tied to it. A rotation into Apple rewards firms that can monetise AI through software and services rather than by pouring billions into hardware, and it revives the recurring question — first raised in the 'DeepSeek moment' — of whether the world really needs ever-more-expensive chips. For everyday investors, it's a reminder that leadership at the top can change fast, and that concentration in a few names cuts both ways.
depth.why_now: The trigger was a rough July for chip stocks: doubts about the durability of the AI trade — sharpened by China's cheap, capable Kimi K3 model and a broad reassessment of AI valuations — knocked nearly 19% off the semiconductor index from its peak, dragging Nvidia down 3.5% on Friday just as Apple held firm. That gap was enough to flip the ranking.
depth.watch_next: Whether either company is first to cross the $5 trillion milestone, and whether the rotation out of chipmakers deepens or reverses when Nvidia and the big AI names report their next earnings.
depth.market_link: Directly repositions Apple (AAPL) above Nvidia (NVDA) by market cap; a read on the whole AI-chip complex (AMD, Micron/MU, TSMC/TSM, ASML) and on the mega-cap-heavy S&P 500 and Nasdaq, where a rotation from capex-heavy chip names toward services-led Apple signals cooling AI-trade risk appetite.

key_terms.definition: The total stock-market value of a company — its share price times the number of shares. It's the standard yardstick for ranking the 'biggest' companies.
Specialised processors (Nvidia's speciality) built to run the heavy maths behind AI; soaring demand for them made Nvidia briefly the world's most valuable firm.
When investors move money out of one group of stocks (here, AI chipmakers) and into another (here, Apple) as their view of where the best returns lie changes.
A widely watched index tracking major chip stocks; its ~19% drop from record highs is shorthand for the AI-chip trade losing steam.

cards.title: A magnitude 7.3 earthquake strikes off the Mexico–Guatemala coast, triggering a brief tsunami alert — but no deaths or major damage
cards.what_happened: On July 17, 2026, at about 7:48 a.m. Pacific time, a powerful earthquake struck in the Pacific Ocean roughly 30 miles (48 km) southwest of Aquiles Serdán, near Mexico's Chiapas coast and the Guatemalan border, according to the US Geological Survey (USGS). It was first reported as magnitude 7.4 before being recalculated to 7.3, at a shallow depth of about 6.2 miles (10 km) — shallow quakes shake the surface harder. A tsunami threat was issued for coasts within about 186 miles of the epicentre and then cancelled roughly three hours later; the largest wave measured only about 1.1 feet above normal tide level at Puerto Madero, Chiapas. Several aftershocks followed, the strongest a magnitude 6.0, and authorities so far report no deaths or major damage.
key_points: 7.3 quake hit ~30 miles off Aquiles Serdán, southern Mexico
Tsunami threat issued for Mexico and Guatemala, then lifted ~3 hours later
Biggest wave was just ~1.1 feet; aftershocks reached magnitude 6.0
No casualties or major damage reported so far
cards.moot: How much harm an earthquake does depends as much on its depth, its distance from people and the quality of local buildings as on its raw magnitude. A strong quake that strikes deep and offshore can pass with barely a scratch, while a weaker one shallow beneath a city can be deadly — which is why preparedness (building codes and warning systems) often matters more than the number on the seismograph.

depth.background: Southern Mexico and Guatemala sit on the 'Pacific Ring of Fire', a horseshoe-shaped belt around the Pacific Ocean where most of the world's earthquakes and volcanic eruptions happen. It is so active because giant slabs of the Earth's crust — tectonic plates — grind and dive beneath one another here; off this coast the Cocos plate is slowly forced under the North American and Caribbean plates, a process called subduction that stores up stress until it snaps as a quake. This is the same region that has produced devastating tremors before, so a big offshore quake immediately raises fears of shaking and tsunamis onshore.
depth.why_it_matters: A magnitude 7.3 is a major earthquake — powerful enough to level poorly built structures and spawn dangerous waves — striking a densely populated, quake-prone stretch of Mexico and Guatemala. That it passed with little harm is itself the story: it shows how much a quake's depth, distance offshore and the strength of local buildings determine whether a big tremor becomes a catastrophe, and it was a live test of the region's tsunami-warning system, which sounded the alarm and stood it down within hours.
depth.ripple_effects: The immediate risk now is aftershocks, which can keep rattling the region for days or weeks and can topple structures already weakened by the main quake, so coastal towns stay on alert. Local authorities will spend the next days checking remote fishing villages and rural areas where damage can go unreported at first. More broadly, the episode is a reminder to the millions living along the Ring of Fire — from Mexico to Japan — that they sit atop one of the planet's most restless zones, keeping pressure on governments to enforce earthquake-resistant building codes and maintain warning systems.
depth.why_now: The quake is a natural release of stress built up along the subduction zone off Chiapas; it dominated headlines because a 7.3 magnitude combined with tsunami warnings across two countries is exactly the kind of event that forces coastal communities to react in real time, even when — as here — the danger quickly fades.
depth.watch_next: Whether strong aftershocks continue, and the damage assessments from remote coastal and mountain communities near the epicentre, where harm can take a day or two to surface.

key_terms.definition: A number measuring an earthquake's energy; the scale is exponential, so a 7.3 releases roughly 30 times more energy than a 6.3 and is classed as a 'major' quake.
A belt around the Pacific Ocean where tectonic plates collide, producing the bulk of the world's earthquakes and volcanoes.
A place where one tectonic plate is forced beneath another; the stress that builds up there is released as some of the planet's largest earthquakes.
A smaller quake that follows a larger one in the same area as the crust readjusts; aftershocks can still be strong enough to cause fresh damage.

cards.title: China's Moonshot AI unveils Kimi K3 — a giant, cheap open model that rivals America's best and triggers a 'DeepSeek moment' chip selloff
cards.what_happened: On July 17, 2026, Moonshot unveiled Kimi K3, a model with about 2.8 trillion parameters — which it says makes it the largest 'open-weight' model in the world (meaning the finished model can be downloaded and run by anyone, not locked behind one company's servers). On public benchmark tests it reportedly matched or beat most leading US models, trailing only Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and OpenAI's GPT-5.6. Crucially, Moonshot priced it at roughly $15 per million 'output tokens' (the units of text a model generates), versus about $50 for Fable 5 — and said it will release the full model weights publicly on July 27. Investors read it as another cheap-but-capable China shock: Hong Kong-listed AI names tumbled (Z.ai fell as much as 30%, MiniMax about 16%, Alibaba around 4%), Bloomberg's Asian semiconductor index dropped more than 6%, and US chip leaders Nvidia, AMD, Micron and TSMC sank as the selloff spread to Wall Street.
key_points: Kimi K3 has 2.8 trillion 'parameters' — the world's largest open-weight AI model
On benchmarks it trails only Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and OpenAI's GPT-5.6
Priced at $15 per million output tokens — about a third of Fable 5's $50
AI and chip stocks slid; Asia's semiconductor index fell more than 6%
cards.moot: When a capable competitor shows up at a fraction of the price, it can reprice an entire industry overnight — because a lot of a market leader's value rests on the belief that its product is scarce and hard to copy. 'Good enough and much cheaper' is often more disruptive than 'the best', and in a gold rush the companies selling the shovels (here, chipmakers) can fall fastest when people suspect the digging just got cheaper.

depth.background: An AI 'model' is the underlying brain behind chatbots like ChatGPT or Claude — software trained on huge amounts of text to answer questions, write and reason. Its rough size is measured in 'parameters', the internal dials it tunes while learning; more parameters can mean more capability, but also far more computing power (and expensive chips) to build and run. Until now the most powerful models came from a few US firms — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google — and were kept closed. Moonshot AI is a Chinese startup (backed by e-commerce giant Alibaba and founded by researcher Yang Zhilin) whose 'Kimi' chatbot is popular in China. This launch echoes the 'DeepSeek moment' of early 2025, when another Chinese lab shocked markets by matching US models at a fraction of the cost.
depth.why_it_matters: The story that props up sky-high AI and chip valuations is that building frontier AI requires ever-more, ever-pricier hardware — so the companies selling that hardware (Nvidia above all) and the few labs that can afford it will keep minting money. A Chinese model that is nearly as good, far cheaper, and openly downloadable challenges that story on both ends: it squeezes the prices US labs can charge, and it raises the fear that world-class AI no longer needs the most expensive chips, which is exactly why semiconductor shares fell hardest.
depth.ripple_effects: For users and businesses, cheaper, open models mean AI keeps getting more affordable — good for anyone building on top of it, bad for the premium prices market leaders hoped to charge. For US labs, it pressures them to justify their cost and cut prices. For chipmakers, it revives the 'do we really need $40,000 chips?' question that dented Nvidia during the DeepSeek scare. Geopolitically, it shows China's AI push is thriving despite US export curbs meant to slow it — and lands just as Beijing rallies a new state-backed AI alliance. Expect debate over whether the benchmark claims hold up once outsiders test the weights on July 27.
depth.why_now: The trigger is the launch itself, but the violent market reaction is really a replay of the DeepSeek shock: investors are primed to sell AI and chip stocks the instant a Chinese lab shows it can rival US models cheaply. It also comes amid an intensifying China–US AI race, with Beijing pushing homegrown models to route around American chip restrictions.
depth.watch_next: The public release of Kimi K3's full weights on July 27 — whether independent testers confirm it really rivals the top US models — and how Nvidia and the big US AI labs respond on pricing.
depth.market_link: Directly hit chipmakers Nvidia (NVDA), AMD, Micron (MU) and TSMC (TSM), chip-gear maker ASML, and Asian AI names like Alibaba (BABA); a broad risk-off move across AI and semiconductor indices, with the read-through being pressure on the premium pricing of US AI leaders.

key_terms.definition: The internal 'dials' a model tunes as it learns; more parameters can mean more capability but need far more computing power. Kimi K3 has about 2.8 trillion.
An AI model whose finished 'brain' can be freely downloaded and run by anyone, rather than being locked behind a single company's paid service.
The chunks of text an AI reads and writes; AI services are priced per million tokens, so 'output tokens' is essentially the cost of the text the model generates.
A reference to early 2025, when Chinese lab DeepSeek matched top US models at a fraction of the cost and briefly wiped hundreds of billions off AI and chip stocks.

cards.title: A cholesterol pill to replace the needle: the US FDA approves Merck's enlicitide, the first pill that works like the powerful injected cholesterol drugs
cards.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) — the agency that decides which medicines can be sold in America — approved Merck's LIPFENDRA (chemical name enlicitide), a 20-milligram tablet taken once a day alongside diet and exercise to lower LDL in adults with high cholesterol. It is the first PCSK9 inhibitor that works as a pill rather than a shot. Merck engineered enlicitide as a special looped ('macrocyclic') peptide that survives the gut and can be swallowed. In its main late-stage trial it cut LDL cholesterol by about 59% at 24 weeks versus a placebo — in the same league as the injected versions. Merck said it plans to sell the drug for about $10.50 a day, roughly $315 for a month's supply.
key_points: FDA approves Merck's LIPFENDRA (enlicitide) — a once-daily tablet
First-ever oral 'PCSK9 inhibitor', a class that until now had to be injected
Cut 'bad' LDL cholesterol by about 59% in its main trial
Priced at ~$10.50 a day; rivals the injected drugs Repatha and Praluent
cards.moot: How a medicine is delivered can matter as much as how well it works. A drug that must be injected reaches far fewer people than an equally effective pill, because convenience drives whether patients actually keep taking it. Turning a hard-to-take treatment into an easy one is itself a form of medical progress — sometimes a bigger one than the underlying chemistry.

depth.background: Cholesterol is a waxy substance in your blood; too much of the 'bad' kind — LDL — builds up inside arteries and is a leading cause of heart attacks and strokes, the biggest killers in the world. The standard cheap defence is statins (pills like atorvastatin), but many people can't get their LDL low enough on statins alone. For them, drugmakers created a far more powerful class of medicine called PCSK9 inhibitors: they block a protein (PCSK9) that stops the liver from clearing LDL out of the blood, so switching it off lets the liver mop up much more cholesterol. The catch was that these drugs — Amgen's Repatha and Regeneron/Sanofi's Praluent — are large antibody molecules that had to be delivered by injection every few weeks, which is inconvenient, more expensive, and puts a lot of patients off.
depth.why_it_matters: Turning a powerful injected medicine into a simple daily pill can dramatically widen who actually takes it: people are far more likely to stick with a tablet than with regular self-injections, and pills are cheaper and easier to distribute. Because heart disease driven by high LDL is the world's leading cause of death, a convenient, potent cholesterol pill has the potential to prevent large numbers of heart attacks and strokes — if it reaches enough people at a price they can afford.
depth.ripple_effects: For patients, it offers a needle-free route to the strong LDL cuts that until now meant injections — especially useful for those statins alone don't help. For the industry, it is a direct challenge to Amgen's Repatha and Regeneron/Sanofi's Praluent, and it strengthens Merck's hand as some of its older blockbusters (like the cancer drug Keytruda) approach patent expiry. Insurers and health systems will now wrestle with cost: at ~$315 a month it is far pricier than a generic statin, so debates over who qualifies and what gets covered will shape how many people benefit. And its success validates the harder science of making 'biologic-strength' drugs swallowable — a template other companies will chase.
depth.why_now: The approval is the payoff from years of large clinical trials (Merck's 'CORALreef' programme) that had to prove the pill was both safe and as effective as the injections; clearing the FDA's review is what turns that research into a product doctors can actually prescribe, which is why it is news today.
depth.watch_next: Whether ongoing outcome trials confirm the pill actually cuts heart attacks and deaths (not just cholesterol numbers), how insurers price and cover it, and how Amgen and Regeneron/Sanofi respond to defend their injected drugs.
depth.market_link: Directly positive for Merck (MRK); a competitive threat to Amgen (AMGN), whose Repatha, and Regeneron (REGN)/Sanofi (SNY), whose Praluent, are the injected PCSK9 drugs this pill rivals.

key_terms.definition: The 'bad' cholesterol that builds up in artery walls; high levels raise the risk of heart attacks and strokes, so lowering it is a central goal of heart-disease prevention.
A powerful class of cholesterol medicine that blocks the PCSK9 protein, letting the liver clear far more LDL from the blood. Until now these drugs had to be injected.
The common, cheap first-line pill (e.g. atorvastatin) that lowers cholesterol; many patients still can't reach their target on statins alone.
The US regulator's decision that a medicine is safe and effective enough to be sold — the gate a new drug must pass before doctors can prescribe it.

cards.title: US widens its war on Iran — bombing near Tehran for the first time and disabling a tanker, as Iran fires back at American bases in the Gulf
cards.what_happened: Early on Thursday, July 16, 2026, the US widened its air campaign, striking targets around the capital Tehran for the first time — after US Central Command (CENTCOM) disabled a Curaçao-flagged oil tanker, the Belma, that it said ignored warnings and tried to run its blockade toward Kharg Island, Iran's main oil-export terminal. In retaliation, Iran's Revolutionary Guard launched missiles at US military sites across the Gulf: Bahrain, home to the US Fifth Fleet headquarters; a logistics hub at Mina Abdullah in Kuwait; and Jordan, whose military said it shot down three Iranian ballistic missiles. Iran's government says more than 30 civilians have been killed and over 260 injured in the US strikes so far.
key_points: US expands strikes into Iran, hitting near Tehran for the first time
US disables the tanker 'Belma' for trying to run its naval blockade
Iran retaliates on US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan
30+ civilians reported killed; oil climbs to a one-month high
cards.moot: Geographic chokepoints are strategic weapons: whoever controls a narrow passage like Hormuz can throttle a huge share of global trade, so a regional conflict there sends prices and risk rippling worldwide. Energy security and geopolitics are inseparable — a war few people can see up close still shows up in the fuel everyone buys.

depth.background: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea passage between Iran and the Arabian Peninsula through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil is shipped every day — one of the planet's most important 'chokepoints'. The US and Iran have been locked in an escalating military confrontation, and this week President Trump ordered a renewed naval blockade of Iran's ports and waves of airstrikes to control shipping through the strait, while Iran vows to resist and calls the strait a 'red line' it will defend 'until the end'.
depth.why_it_matters: American bombs reaching the Iranian capital and Iranian missiles landing on Gulf states mark a sharp escalation that drags more countries toward a wider regional war. And because the fighting is choking the Strait of Hormuz, it threatens the flow of a fifth of the world's oil — which is why crude prices have jumped to a one-month high, a cost that ripples into every economy through pricier fuel and higher inflation.
depth.ripple_effects: Fewer tankers are risking the strait, so oil and shipping-insurance costs are climbing and could feed back into global inflation just as central banks hoped to start cutting interest rates. The Gulf states that host US bases — Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, the UAE — are now direct targets, raising the danger they get pulled into the war themselves. Airlines are rerouting around the region at added fuel cost, and big oil importers like India are exposed (New Delhi has already nearly doubled its windfall tax on fuel exports as prices spike). A prolonged blockade could tip a contained confrontation into a full regional conflict.
depth.why_now: The escalation follows Trump's decision to reinstate a naval blockade and press for control of Hormuz shipping; each side is now answering the other's strikes, and the US move against the Belma tanker — combined with pushing its bombing toward Tehran — prompted Iran to retaliate directly against US bases in neighbouring countries.
depth.watch_next: Whether Iran tries to fully close the Strait of Hormuz to all shipping, and whether Gulf states or Israel are drawn deeper into the fighting.
depth.market_link: Crude oil (Brent and WTI) has jumped to a one-month high; watch energy majors and oil-services stocks rise, airlines and shippers fall on fuel and insurance costs, safe havens like gold and the US dollar catch a bid, and oil-importing currencies such as the Indian rupee stay under pressure.

key_terms.definition: A narrow shipping channel at the mouth of the Gulf that carries about a fifth of the world's seaborne oil; closing it would spike energy prices worldwide.
Using warships to stop vessels from entering or leaving a country's ports — here, the US trying to cut off Iran's sea trade, especially its oil exports.
Iran's largest oil-export terminal, handling the bulk of its crude shipments — making it a prime target and prize in a fight over Iran's oil.
US Central Command, the branch of the US military responsible for operations across the Middle East.

cards.title: Uber makes a $14.8bn move to swallow Delivery Hero — a bid to build the world's biggest food-delivery firm outside China
cards.what_happened: On July 16, 2026 Uber launched a public takeover offer for Delivery Hero, offering shareholders €41.50 per share in cash — an equity value of about $14.8 billion (roughly $13.7bn after Uber's earlier stake purchases). Delivery Hero's management and supervisory boards unanimously backed the bid, and Prosus, its biggest shareholder, agreed to hand over its shares, which would take Uber's economic interest to about 53%. To ease competition worries, a separate buyer, SSW Partners, will take Delivery Hero's businesses in 14 markets where the two already overlap for about $1.6 billion. Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi said the deal would roughly double the number of markets where Uber offers both rides and delivery — from 34 to 58.
key_points: Uber offers €41.50 a share in cash, valuing Delivery Hero at ~$14.8bn
Deal would nearly double Uber's combined ride+delivery markets, 34 to 58
Delivery Hero's board backs it; top shareholder Prosus agrees to sell
Uber offloads 14 overlapping markets to defuse competition concerns
cards.moot: In platform businesses, markets tend to collapse toward one or two dominant players — so after a long, loss-making land grab, the endgame is consolidation: the profitable survivor buys the rest to own the network. And big acquirers now design deals to pass antitrust review, pre-selling the overlapping pieces before regulators even ask.

depth.background: Delivery Hero is a Berlin-based company that owns a patchwork of regional food-delivery brands — apps like Foodpanda, Talabat and Glovo — across Europe, the Middle East, Asia and Latin America, the kind of service that brings restaurant meals and groceries to your door. Uber, best known for ride-hailing, has spent years turning Uber Eats into a second engine of its business. For a decade the delivery industry has been a race to grab as many cities as possible and then swallow the heavy losses that come from paying drivers and undercutting rivals — so the survivors are now buying each other to reach a size where the business finally makes money.
depth.why_it_matters: This is one of the largest deals ever in the app-delivery world, and it would make the combined Uber the dominant food-delivery player almost everywhere outside China. Consolidation on this scale changes who sets prices and delivery fees for millions of customers and restaurants, and how much bargaining power gig drivers have. It also signals that the 'grow at any cost' era of delivery apps is ending — the money now flows to whoever can be the last big platform standing in each region.
depth.ripple_effects: If regulators in Europe, the Middle East and Asia clear it, Uber gains tens of millions of new users and a ready-made network in regions where it was weak — but it also inherits the punishing economics of low-margin food delivery. Restaurants and shoppers could face less competition, which over time can mean higher fees or commissions. Rivals like DoorDash and Just Eat may feel pressure to bulk up through their own deals. And the SSW carve-out shows how big tech deals now get pre-negotiated with antitrust in mind — selling off overlapping markets up front to win approval.
depth.why_now: Years of losses and investor pressure have pushed delivery apps to merge rather than keep burning cash fighting each other; Delivery Hero, long unprofitable and carrying debt, became a natural target, and Uber — now consistently profitable — has the firepower to buy scale. Prosus's willingness to sell its large stake is what made a clean deal possible today.
depth.watch_next: Watch whether EU and other antitrust regulators demand deeper divestitures — and whether enough Delivery Hero shareholders tender their shares to let Uber actually close the deal.
depth.market_link: Uber (UBER) and Delivery Hero (DHER) shares are the direct movers; Prosus (PRX) monetises a big stake, while rivals such as DoorDash (DASH) and Just Eat Takeaway trade on the read-through for delivery-sector consolidation.

key_terms.definition: A public offer to buy a company by asking its shareholders to sell ('tender') their shares, usually at a set cash price each.
A large Dutch-listed tech investment group (spun out of South Africa's Naspers) that was Delivery Hero's biggest shareholder.
Selling off part of a business — here, the overlapping markets Uber agrees to give up so competition regulators will approve the deal.

cards.title: The EU orders Google to crack open Android and Search — rival AI assistants get 'Hey Google'-style access and competitors get its search data
cards.what_happened: On Thursday, July 16, 2026, the European Commission — the EU's executive arm — issued two legally binding 'specification decisions' against Google under the DMA. The first forces Google to open up 11 features of Android so rival AI assistants (think OpenAI's ChatGPT or Anthropic's Claude) can tap the same phone functions Google's own Gemini assistant uses — meaning you could summon a competing assistant by voice, the way you say 'Hey Google', to book a taxi or look something up. The second orders Google to hand rival search engines and AI chatbot makers anonymised Search data — the ranking, query, click and view information that powers good search results — on 'fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory' (FRAND) terms. The data-sharing must begin by January 2027, and the Android changes arrive in the next version of the system from July 2027.
key_points: EU issues two binding orders forcing Google to open Android and Search to rivals
11 Android features open up so rival AI assistants work by voice, like 'Hey Google'
Google must share anonymised search data with rival engines and AI chatbots
Search-data sharing starts Jan 2027; the Android changes land from July 2027
cards.moot: In digital markets, the real moat isn't a single product — it's control of the gateway (the operating system, the search box) plus the mountain of data that flows through it. That is exactly why regulators are shifting from breaking up companies to forcing them to share access and data: prying open the gateway can do more for competition than any fine.

depth.background: For years a handful of tech giants — Google, Apple, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft — have controlled the 'gateways' most people use to reach the internet: app stores, search engines, phone operating systems. Google is dominant on two of them at once: it runs Android, the operating system on the large majority of the world's smartphones, and Google Search, which handles the bulk of the world's web searches. To curb that power, the European Union passed the Digital Markets Act (DMA) — a 2022 law that labels the biggest platforms 'gatekeepers' and forces them to let competitors plug into their systems on fair terms, rather than quietly favouring their own products. This week's orders are Brussels spelling out, feature by feature, exactly what Google now has to open up.
depth.why_it_matters: This strikes at the core of how Google's dominance sustains itself: rivals have long argued they can't build a competitive search engine or phone assistant because they can't get the scale of data and the deep system access that Google reserves for itself. Forcing Google to share both could, for the first time, give challengers — especially AI chatbot makers racing to become your default 'answer engine' — a real shot at competing on Google's home turf. It also sets a template: the EU is showing it will regulate not just yesterday's search box but tomorrow's AI assistants, and where Europe leads on tech rules, other regions often follow.
depth.ripple_effects: If it works as intended, phone users in Europe could soon pick a non-Google AI assistant as their everyday helper, and smaller search engines could deliver sharper results using Google's shared data — chipping away at Google's ad-fuelled money machine, since search ads are its biggest profit source. Google will likely warn that sharing data raises privacy and security risks and may appeal or drag its feet, as gatekeepers often do. Rivals from DuckDuckGo to OpenAI and Perplexity stand to gain the most. And because building one version of Android for Europe and another for everyone else is costly, some of these openings may eventually spread worldwide.
depth.why_now: The orders cap months of technical proceedings under the DMA, and they land just as the AI-assistant war is heating up — regulators want the rules in place before Google's Gemini can lock in its advantage on Android the way Google Search once locked in the desktop. The timing reflects a broader EU push this year to enforce its digital rulebook against every major US platform at once.
depth.watch_next: Whether Google appeals the decisions in the EU courts, and how it actually implements the 11 Android openings when the changes are due to reach users from July 2027.
depth.market_link: A modest regulatory headwind for Alphabet (GOOGL), whose search-ad dominance is the target; a potential tailwind for search and AI challengers such as Microsoft (MSFT)/OpenAI, Perplexity and DuckDuckGo, and a read-through for how the EU may treat other 'gatekeepers' like Apple, Amazon and Meta.

key_terms.definition: A 2022 EU law that designates the biggest tech platforms as 'gatekeepers' and forces them to let competitors interoperate with their systems, instead of favouring their own services.
The EU's label for a dominant platform that controls a key gateway to consumers — like an app store, search engine or operating system — and so faces extra obligations.
A standard requiring a company to offer access — here, to Google's search data — on even-handed terms it can't rig to disadvantage rivals.
The ability of different products to work together — for example, a rival AI assistant plugging into the same Android voice controls Google's own assistant uses.

cards.title: Zelensky fires Ukraine's popular 'drone-war' defence minister — and mass protests erupt in Kyiv as parliament installs a new prime minister
cards.what_happened: On July 15–16, 2026, Zelensky moved to dismiss Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov following a meeting with military leaders, and gave no public explanation for the decision. The move stunned Ukrainians and set off street protests in Kyiv and other cities, with civil-society groups and soldiers voicing anger at losing the minister they credit with the drone campaign. At the same time, parliament approved a broader government reshuffle: Serhii Koretskyi, a figure from the energy sector, was confirmed as the new prime minister, replacing Yulia Svyrydenko — Zelensky pointed to Koretskyi's experience preparing the country's power grid for winter.
key_points: Zelensky abruptly sacked defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov — no reason given
Fedorov is popular: he built the drone war that blunted Russia
Crowds rallied across Kyiv and other cities against the ouster
Parliament approved Serhii Koretskyi as new PM in a wider reshuffle
cards.moot: Even under wartime rule, a leader's power ultimately rests on public trust — and removing a popular, successful official without explaining why can spark the very instability the move was meant to avoid. Transparency isn't a courtesy in a crisis; it's what keeps authority intact when people can't simply vote you out.

depth.background: Ukraine has been fighting off Russia's full-scale invasion since February 2022 — the war is now well past its 1,600th day. Because the country is under martial law (emergency wartime rule), normal elections are suspended, so its leadership can only change through President Volodymyr Zelensky and parliament, not the ballot box. Mykhailo Fedorov had become one of the war's most admired figures: a young, tech-savvy minister widely credited with turning cheap, mass-produced drones into a weapon that repeatedly let Ukraine outfight Russia's bigger and costlier military.
depth.why_it_matters: Changing your defence minister in the middle of a war — and especially the person most credited with your biggest military success — is a jarring, high-risk move, and doing it with no stated reason has shaken public trust at a delicate moment. The backlash is a sign that Zelensky's wartime authority, long treated as beyond question, is now being openly challenged from within by the very people doing the fighting.
depth.ripple_effects: A leadership shake-up can unsettle Ukraine's Western backers, who are mid-negotiation on drone deals and waiting on licences for US-made Patriot air-defence missiles — allies prefer continuity in the officials they deal with. If the protests grow, they pull attention and national unity away from the front line, which is exactly what Moscow hopes for. The new prime minister's energy background hints the government is already bracing for another winter of Russian strikes on power plants. And the sacking raises an unanswered question — whether it reflects a policy clash, a personality dispute, or a power struggle inside Zelensky's inner circle.
depth.why_now: The immediate trigger was a military-leadership meeting after which Zelensky decided on the change, timed alongside a wider cabinet reshuffle he framed as 'refreshing' the government for the winter war effort — but the total absence of any stated reason is what turned a routine reshuffle into a public firestorm.
depth.watch_next: Whether the protests grow or fade, who Zelensky names as the new defence minister, and whether Western partners signal any wobble in drone and Patriot-missile support.
depth.market_link: Ukraine isn't a market itself, but political turbulence in a country mid-war can nudge European risk sentiment and defence and drone-maker shares, and any wobble in Western arms support feeds into how markets price the broader Ukraine-Russia war risk.

key_terms.definition: Emergency wartime rule that concentrates power in the government and suspends normal elections — which is why Ukraine's leaders change by appointment, not by a public vote, during the war.
The civilian official who runs a country's armed forces, weapons purchases and military policy.
Advanced US-made air-defence missiles that shoot down incoming aircraft and ballistic missiles — a top priority for Ukraine against Russian air strikes.

cards.title: Axis Bank's profit jumps 23% to ₹7,114 crore and bad loans shrink — a reassuring quarter from one of India's biggest private banks
cards.what_happened: On July 18, 2026, Axis Bank reported that its standalone net profit for the June quarter rose about 23% from a year earlier to ₹7,114 crore, ahead of analysts' expectations. Its net interest income (NII) — the core profit from lending, i.e. the gap between what it earns on loans and pays out on deposits — climbed 8% to ₹14,646 crore, while its net interest margin (NIM), that gap expressed as a percentage, stood at 3.46%. Crucially, asset quality improved: gross non-performing assets (loans unlikely to be repaid) fell to 1.28% of total loans from 1.57% a year earlier, and net NPAs eased to 0.39%. The bank's overall balance sheet grew 20% to roughly ₹19.2 lakh crore (about ₹19.2 trillion).
key_points: Standalone net profit rose 23% year-on-year to ₹7,114 crore, beating forecasts
Net interest income up 8% to ₹14,646 crore; margin (NIM) at 3.46%
Bad loans (gross NPAs) fell to 1.28% from 1.57% a year earlier
Balance sheet grew 20% to about ₹19.2 lakh crore
cards.moot: A bank's profit is only half the story — the other half is the quality of its loans. Rising earnings paired with shrinking bad loans is the healthy combination, because a bank can always boost profits temporarily by lending recklessly, only to pay for it later when those loans go bad. Watching non-performing assets alongside profit tells you whether the growth is real and durable.

depth.background: Axis Bank is one of India's largest private-sector banks, and its quarterly results are watched as a health-check on both the bank and the wider economy — because banks make money by lending, their profits rise and fall with how much people and businesses are borrowing and how reliably they repay. Every three months (a 'quarter') listed companies publish their financial scorecard; the April–June period is 'Q1' of India's financial year. Investors zero in on a handful of numbers: how much the bank earned, how fat its lending margin is, and how many loans are going sour — the last being the classic warning light for any bank.
depth.why_it_matters: A big private bank posting double-digit profit growth while its bad loans shrink is a good sign for India's financial system — it suggests borrowers are largely keeping up with repayments even as global risks (a spiking oil price, a weak rupee, inflation just above the RBI's target) cloud the outlook. Bank results also move the market heavily because lenders are among the largest weights in India's Sensex and Nifty indices, so a strong showing from a heavyweight like Axis can lift sentiment across the board.
depth.ripple_effects: Steady margins and falling bad loans give the bank room to keep lending, which supports credit flowing to homebuyers, businesses and consumers. Strong results from Axis raise the bar and set the tone for peers like HDFC Bank, ICICI and Kotak reporting around the same time, shaping how investors judge the whole banking sector this earnings season. But the numbers also carry a caution flag for the quarters ahead: with inflation now above the Reserve Bank of India's comfort zone and the RBI governor flagging the West Asia (Middle East) conflict and an uncertain monsoon as growth risks, banks' margins could get squeezed if interest rates or deposit costs shift.
depth.why_now: This is a scheduled quarterly earnings release, the first of India's 2026-27 financial year — the standard moment banks open their books. Its significance is sharpened by the backdrop: it lands the same week India's inflation breached the RBI's 4% target and the rupee stayed under pressure from higher oil prices, making a clean, profitable quarter a welcome data point.
depth.watch_next: How rival private banks (HDFC Bank, ICICI, Kotak) fare in their Q1 results, and whether banks' lending margins hold up if the RBI shifts course on interest rates amid rising inflation.
depth.market_link: Moves Axis Bank (AXISBANK) shares and sets the tone for India's bank-heavy Nifty and Sensex; a read-through for private-sector peers HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank and Kotak Mahindra reporting this earnings season, and a gauge of Indian credit health as inflation runs above the RBI's target.

key_terms.definition: A bank's core earnings from lending — the difference between the interest it collects on loans and the interest it pays on deposits.
That lending profit expressed as a percentage of the bank's assets; a higher NIM means the bank keeps more from each rupee it lends. Axis reported 3.46%.
A loan the borrower has stopped repaying, so the bank may never get its money back. A falling NPA ratio means loan quality is improving.
A three-month slice of the financial year; Q1 in India covers April–June. Companies report results each quarter as a running scorecard.

cards.title: US senators push a bill to slap 100% tariffs on India for buying Russian oil — New Delhi says it's 'closely following'
cards.what_happened: A bipartisan group of more than 60 US senators is backing the 'Senator Lindsey O. Graham Sanctioning Russia Act of 2026', introduced by Senator Richard Blumenthal alongside Graham's widow, Darline Graham. The revised bill would let the US president impose tariffs of up to 100% on imports from the five biggest purchasers of Russian oil and gas — India, China, Slovakia, Hungary and Azerbaijan — while carving out an exception for countries that import less than 15% of Russia's gas and are cutting back. It scales down an earlier, blunter proposal of 500% tariffs and gives the president discretion to waive the penalties in the US national interest; it has the backing of Senate Majority Leader John Thune and President Trump. At its July 17 briefing, India's Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal said New Delhi is 'closely following' the legislation, adding that India's oil purchases are guided by its energy-security needs and that it sources crude from many suppliers.
key_points: Bipartisan US bill would let Trump hit India with up to 100% tariffs
Targets the top 5 buyers of Russian oil: India, China, Slovakia, Hungary, Azerbaijan
60+ senators back it and President Trump supports it
India's foreign ministry: 'closely following', cites its energy-security needs
cards.moot: Modern economic pressure increasingly works through third parties: rather than only sanctioning an adversary directly, powers threaten that country's trading partners, turning them into pressure points to isolate the target. It's also a reminder that cheap energy can carry a hidden geopolitical price — a bargain barrel today can become a diplomatic liability tomorrow.

depth.background: When Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022, the West hit Moscow with sanctions and largely stopped buying its oil, so Russia was forced to sell its crude at a steep discount. India — which imports most of the oil it burns and had bought almost none from Russia before the war — jumped at the cheap barrels, and Russian crude has since grown to roughly a third of India's oil imports, making India one of the world's biggest buyers. Western governments see that flow of oil money as funding Russia's war, and have been looking for ways to choke it off — including 'secondary' measures that punish not Russia itself, but the third countries still buying its oil. This bill is the latest, and it is named after the late US Senator Lindsey Graham, a long-time champion of tough Russia sanctions.
depth.why_it_matters: The United States is one of India's largest export markets, so a 100% tariff would roughly double the price of Indian goods sold in America — a potential body-blow to Indian exporters of everything from smartphones and machinery to textiles, gems and generic medicines. In effect, Washington is threatening India's access to the US market to force it to give up cheap Russian oil, putting New Delhi in a bind between two things it values: discounted energy that keeps fuel and inflation in check, and a booming trade relationship with the US.
depth.ripple_effects: The threat lands right in the middle of delicate India-US trade talks and gives Washington fresh leverage. If India were pushed to buy less Russian crude, it would likely pay more for oil elsewhere — swelling its import bill, pressuring the rupee, and adding to inflation that has already pushed past the Reserve Bank of India's comfort zone. Indian refiners such as Reliance, IOC, BPCL and HPCL, which have leaned heavily on cheap Russian barrels, would see margins squeezed. But this is still a bill, not a law: it must pass both chambers of Congress and be signed, and even then the president could waive the tariffs — so for now it works as pressure and a bargaining chip as much as a firm penalty.
depth.why_now: The measure has gathered momentum as the Ukraine war grinds on and crude prices spike amid the US-Iran conflict at the Strait of Hormuz, sharpening Western resolve to cut Russia's oil revenue. It crossed the 60-cosponsor mark this week, and India's MEA was pressed on it at its July 17 media briefing — putting the threat squarely on New Delhi's radar.
depth.watch_next: Whether the bill gets a full Senate floor vote, and any signal from Trump on whether he would actually use — or waive — the tariff power against India.
depth.market_link: A risk for Indian exporters (IT services, pharma, autos, gems & jewellery, textiles) and for refiners that process Russian crude such as Reliance (RELIANCE), IOC, BPCL and HPCL; pressure on the rupee (USDINR), and broadly supportive of global oil prices if big buyers are forced to shift away from Russian barrels.

key_terms.definition: Penalties aimed not at the sanctioned country itself (Russia) but at third countries that keep trading with it — here, taxing imports from nations that buy Russian oil.
India's foreign ministry, which handles the country's diplomacy and speaks for the government on international issues.
A lawmaker who formally adds their name in support of a bill; a high count (here 60+) signals it has broad backing and a better chance of passing.
Oil Russia sells below world prices because Western buyers shun it; India buying it cheaply is what makes it a target of this bill.

cards.title: Reliance's profit 'falls 25%' — but only because of a one-off a year ago: revenue still jumped 25% as oil, Jio and retail powered India's biggest company
cards.what_happened: On July 17, 2026, RIL reported results for Q1 FY27 (the April–June quarter). Consolidated net profit — the group's total bottom-line earnings — came in around ₹23,001 crore, down roughly 25% from about ₹30,681 crore a year earlier (the portion attributable to RIL's own shareholders was about ₹20,946 crore). But that headline fall is misleading: revenue actually rose 25.4% to ₹3,11,850 crore, and operating profit (EBITDA — earnings before interest, tax and accounting write-downs, a cleaner gauge of the core business) climbed about 10% to ₹51,403 crore. The reason profit 'fell' is a base effect: the year-ago quarter was flattered by a one-time ₹8,924 crore gain from selling listed investments, which won't repeat. Strip that out and underlying earnings grew, led by a ~30% jump in the oil-to-chemicals division.
key_points: Q1 net profit about ₹23,001 crore, down ~25% from a year earlier
But revenue rose 25% to ₹3.12 lakh crore — the business actually grew
The 'drop' is a base effect: last year's quarter had a ₹8,924 crore one-off gain
Oil-to-chemicals sales up ~30%; operating profit (EBITDA) up ~10%
cards.moot: A falling profit number isn't always bad news — you have to compare like with like. One-time gains can inflate a past quarter and make normal, healthy growth look like a decline. When a headline profit and the revenue trend point in opposite directions, the revenue and operating-profit (EBITDA) lines usually tell the truer story of whether a business is actually growing.

depth.background: Reliance Industries (RIL) is India's most valuable company and its results move the whole stock market, because it is the single biggest weight in the Sensex and Nifty (the two headline indices that track India's top companies). RIL is really three businesses in one: 'O2C' — oil refining and petrochemicals, turning crude into fuels and plastics; Jio, the country's largest mobile-phone and broadband network; and Reliance Retail, its huge chain of stores. Every quarter (a three-month reporting period) it publishes its profit and sales, and investors comb through them for the health of the Indian consumer, telecom and energy.
depth.why_it_matters: Because RIL is so large, its results tug the entire index — so how the market reads them affects millions of investors' portfolios and mutual funds. Just as important, this is a textbook lesson in not trusting a scary headline number: a '25% profit crash' actually hides a business that grew its sales and operating profit by double digits. The dip is an accounting mirage created by a one-off in the comparison quarter, not a sign the company is shrinking.
depth.ripple_effects: For the Sensex and Nifty, a clean underlying quarter from their heaviest stock is reassuring even if the headline looks ugly. For consumers and rivals, strong Jio and retail numbers signal Indians are still spending on data and shopping. The O2C surge ties RIL's fortunes to global oil — and with crude spiking on the US–Iran war at the Strait of Hormuz, refining margins are in focus. And investors will keep hunting for clues on the long-awaited stock-market listings (IPOs) of Jio and Reliance Retail, which could unlock huge value.
depth.why_now: This is a scheduled quarterly earnings release, but it lands at a charged moment: oil prices are elevated because of the Iran conflict (which flows straight into RIL's refining business), and Indian markets are jittery after inflation breached the RBI's target and the rupee weakened past 96 — so a bellwether like RIL is watched even more closely.
depth.watch_next: Management's commentary on Jio tariff hikes, retail expansion and any timeline for the Jio and Reliance Retail IPOs — plus how refining margins hold up while oil stays volatile.
depth.market_link: Directly moves Reliance Industries (RIL) — the heaviest stock in the Nifty 50 and Sensex, so it tugs both indices; a read-through for refiners and petrochemicals (oil prices), telecom (Jio) and organised retail.

key_terms.definition: The total bottom-line earnings of a company and all the businesses it controls, added together — RIL's covers O2C, Jio and Retail as one group.
A profit from a one-time event — here, selling investments — that boosts a single quarter but won't recur, so it distorts year-on-year comparisons.
When a change looks big or small mainly because of an unusual figure in the earlier period you're comparing against, not because of what happened now.
Earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation — a measure of operating profit that strips out financing and accounting effects to show how the core business is doing.

cards.title: India's biggest mutual-fund house goes public: SBI Funds Management's ₹9,813-crore IPO closes ~42 times oversubscribed, with big institutions bidding ~140x
cards.what_happened: SBI Funds Management's IPO ran from July 14–16, 2026, priced at ₹545–574 per share for a total size of about ₹9,813 crore, and closed on July 16 heavily oversubscribed — bids came in for roughly 42 times the shares on offer. The demand was lopsided: the slice reserved for large institutional investors (QIBs — qualified institutional buyers, like banks, insurers and foreign funds) was subscribed about 140 times, the wealthy 'non-institutional' (NII/HNI) portion about 22 times, while ordinary retail investors bid about 3.8 times their quota. Grey-market indicators pointed to a listing price around ₹669, a premium of roughly 16% over the top of the price band. Shares are due to be allotted around July 17 and to start trading on the NSE and BSE on July 21.
key_points: SBI Funds Management's IPO closed on July 16, ~41.7x (≈42x) oversubscribed
Institutional (QIB) portion drew a huge ~140x demand; wealthy (NII) ~22x, retail ~3.8x
A ₹9,813-crore, all offer-for-sale issue at ₹545–574 a share
It runs SBI Mutual Fund, India's largest fund house; lists July 21
cards.moot: How a share sale is structured tells you where the money flows. In an 'offer for sale', the cash goes to the owners cashing out, not to the company — so a wildly oversubscribed IPO can be a great deal for the sellers while the business itself gets nothing new to grow with. And when institutions bid far more eagerly than retail investors, it's a clue about which theme — here, Indians pouring savings into mutual funds — the smart money is betting on.

depth.background: A mutual fund pools money from many small investors and a professional manager invests it in stocks or bonds — for millions of Indians, the monthly 'SIP' (systematic investment plan) into a mutual fund is their main way of investing. SBI Funds Management is the company that runs SBI Mutual Fund, the country's largest fund house by assets, and is a joint venture between State Bank of India (India's biggest bank) and France's Amundi, Europe's largest asset manager. An IPO (initial public offering) is the first time a company sells shares to the public and lists on the stock exchange. This one is structured entirely as an 'offer for sale' (OFS), meaning existing owners — SBI and Amundi — are selling part of their stakes and pocketing the money; the company itself raises no new cash.
depth.why_it_matters: Getting bid for 42 times the shares available — with institutions piling in at 140 times — is a loud vote of confidence in India's booming savings-into-markets story: as more Indians move money out of bank deposits and into mutual funds, the companies that manage those funds are seen as a direct, fast-growing bet on that shift. It also puts a public market price on SBI's crown-jewel asset-management arm, and adds another marquee name to a busy run of large Indian IPOs.
depth.ripple_effects: Because it is an offer for sale, the money goes to SBI and Amundi, not into the business — so for parent State Bank of India it unlocks the value of a prized subsidiary and books a gain, which can support the bank's capital. A strong listing would encourage other financial firms to go public and keep India's IPO pipeline hot. The frenzied institutional demand versus thinner retail interest is itself a signal — big money is chasing the 'financialisation of savings' theme hard. But a heavily oversubscribed, richly priced OFS also carries the usual caution: much of the optimism may already be baked into the price, so early gains can be volatile.
depth.why_now: The listing rides a multi-year surge in Indian mutual-fund investing — record monthly SIP inflows have swelled the assets these firms manage — making this a well-timed moment for SBI and Amundi to sell a slice at a strong valuation, and for a market hungry for large, brand-name financial IPOs to lap it up.
depth.watch_next: The July 21 listing — whether the shares open near the ~16% grey-market premium or fade — and the final allotment on July 17.
depth.market_link: A direct read-through for State Bank of India (SBIN), the selling parent, and a sentiment gauge for listed asset managers and the broader 'financialisation of savings' trade; watch SBI Funds Management's July 21 debut on the NSE/BSE.

key_terms.definition: The first time a company sells its shares to the public and lists on a stock exchange, letting anyone buy in and setting a market price.
An IPO in which existing shareholders sell their own shares, so the proceeds go to them — not into the company, which raises no fresh capital.

'Oversubscribed 42x' means investors asked for 42 times the shares on offer. QIBs (qualified institutional buyers) are big professional investors like banks, insurers and foreign funds; their portion here drew ~140x demand.
A firm that runs mutual funds — pooling investors' money and managing it for a fee. SBI Funds Management is India's largest.

cards.title: Wipro's profit goes flat and its outlook stays gloomy — India's IT giant signals the sector's slump isn't over as AI reshapes the business
cards.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, Wipro reported April-June (its fiscal first quarter) net profit of ₹3,352 crore — flat versus a year earlier and down 4.7% from the previous quarter, just short of the roughly ₹3,460 crore analysts expected. Revenue rose 10.6% from a year ago to ₹24,480 crore, but its core IT-services business — the number investors watch most — barely grew, up about 1% on the year and actually down 1.4% quarter-on-quarter. Operating margin slipped 130 basis points (1.3 percentage points) to 16%, squeezed by employee pay rises and spending on new AI capabilities; the finance chief admitted the company's target margin of 17-18% is out of reach for the next few quarters. Crucially, Wipro guided for muted revenue in the current quarter, and declared an interim dividend of ₹2 per share.
key_points: Q1 net profit ₹3,352 crore — flat on last year, down 4.7% on the quarter
Just missed analysts' forecast of about ₹3,460 crore
Guides for flat-to-weak IT-services revenue next quarter
Declares a ₹2-per-share interim dividend; margins slip to 16%
cards.moot: A single company's results are a window onto a whole industry — and onto a bigger shift. Wipro's flat profit and cautious tone show how a business built on selling human hours gets squeezed twice over: once when clients cut budgets, and again when a new technology like AI threatens to do some of that work itself. The firms that thrive will be the ones that sell the AI rather than get replaced by it.

depth.background: Wipro is one of India's 'Big Four' IT-services companies — alongside TCS, Infosys and HCLTech — the firms that write software and run the back-office technology for banks, retailers and manufacturers around the world, mostly in the US and Europe. This industry is a pillar of India's economy: it earns huge amounts of foreign currency, employs millions of engineers, and its quarterly results are treated as a health check on both global corporate spending and Indian markets. For the past two years the sector has been in a slump — nervous Western clients, worried about high interest rates and a shaky economy, have frozen or trimmed their tech budgets — and now a fresh threat looms: generative AI, which can automate some of the very coding and support work these firms sell, forcing them to reinvent what they offer.
depth.why_it_matters: As one of the first big Indian IT firms to report each season, Wipro's numbers set the tone for the whole sector and often move the Nifty IT index. Flat profit plus a cautious forecast tells investors the long-hoped-for recovery in global tech spending still hasn't arrived — and that the margin squeeze from paying staff more while investing in AI is real. It's an early read on whether India's showcase export industry can turn artificial intelligence from a threat into a new source of growth, or whether it keeps eating into the old business faster than new work replaces it.
depth.ripple_effects: A weak Wipro print and soft guidance can drag on peers TCS, Infosys and HCLTech and on the broader Nifty IT index, since investors read one company's caution as a signal for the group. For the hundreds of thousands of engineers these firms employ, a prolonged slump means fewer hires, smaller raises and more pressure to reskill for AI work. Because the sector is India's biggest source of services export earnings, a durable slowdown also matters for the rupee and the country's trade balance. And it feeds a bigger debate: whether AI will ultimately shrink the headcount-heavy 'bodies on projects' model that built India's $250bn-plus IT industry.
depth.why_now: The report lands at the start of the July earnings season, when India's IT majors report one after another, so it's the market's first hard data point on how the sector fared last quarter. It arrives against a backdrop of still-cautious Western clients and mounting questions about how fast generative AI will reshape — and possibly shrink — the traditional IT-outsourcing business.
depth.watch_next: The upcoming results and guidance from TCS, Infosys and HCLTech, which will confirm whether Wipro's muted outlook reflects a sector-wide slump or a company-specific stumble.
depth.market_link: Directly moves Wipro (WIPRO) shares and the Nifty IT index; a read-through for TCS, Infosys and HCLTech ahead of their results, and a signpost for the rupee given IT's role as India's top services exporter.

key_terms.definition: Money earned from building and running software and technology systems for clients — the core business of firms like Wipro, and the figure investors watch most closely.
The share of revenue left as profit after day-to-day running costs; a falling margin means the business is keeping less of each rupee it earns.
One-hundredth of a percentage point. Wipro's margin fell 130 basis points — that is, by 1.3 percentage points.
A company's own forecast for its coming performance; 'muted guidance' means management expects little or no growth ahead.

cards.title: Deadly crush at Puri's Rath Yatra again: at least one devotee dead and 100+ hospitalised in a crowd surge — a year after a stampede here killed three
cards.what_happened: On Thursday, July 16, 2026, a stampede-like crowd surge broke out among devotees near the Singhadwara/Marichikund area as the chariots were being readied and pulled. At least one devotee died — reported to be from suffocation in the crush — while news outlets carried varying figures, with some reporting additional deaths that were not immediately confirmed. Over 100 people (some reports said up to around 200) were taken to hospital with suffocation, fainting and injuries, worsened by heavy rain and the packed conditions. Authorities said nearly 13,000 police personnel, including senior officers, had been deployed to manage the crowds — yet the surge still occurred. The death toll and exact cause were still being established as of this report.
key_points: A crowd surge hit the Rath Yatra at Puri's Singhadwara (Lion's Gate) area
At least one devotee died — reports of the toll vary; 100+ hospitalised
Nearly 13,000 police were deployed, yet the crush still happened
Comes a year after a June 2025 stampede at the same festival killed three
cards.moot: At mass gatherings the crowd itself is the risk: past a certain density, a single stumble or a broken barricade can cascade into a fatal crush, and packing more police in doesn't help if the space and the flow of people aren't designed for the numbers. Preventing disasters is about managing density and exits in advance, not reacting once the surge has begun.

depth.background: The Rath Yatra ('chariot festival') in Puri, Odisha, is one of Hinduism's biggest annual events: the deities Lord Jagannath, his brother Balabhadra and sister Subhadra are pulled through the streets on three towering wooden chariots by hundreds of thousands of devotees, who believe helping to haul the ropes brings blessings. The sheer density of the crowd — packed into narrow lanes around the Jagannath Temple's Singhadwara, or 'Lion's Gate' — makes crowd control extremely hard. This is a recurring danger: just last year, on June 29, 2025, a stampede during the same festival killed three people and injured dozens, after which Odisha's chief minister called the negligence 'unpardonable.'
depth.why_it_matters: A second fatal crush at the very same festival in consecutive years turns a tragic accident into a pattern — and a pointed question about whether crowd-safety lessons from 2025 were actually put into practice. It is a stark reminder that at mass religious gatherings, the crowd itself becomes the hazard: once bodies are packed tightly enough, a small stumble or a collapsed barricade can trigger a deadly domino effect that no amount of faith or policing can instantly stop.
depth.ripple_effects: Expect immediate political fallout in Odisha — condolences, compensation for victims' families, an official inquiry, and opposition attacks over preparedness — echoing the response after the 2025 deaths. The incident will renew a nationwide debate about crowd management at India's giant religious festivals (from the Kumbh Mela to temple town events), where similar crushes recur. Temple and district administrations nationwide may face pressure to adopt stricter crowd-flow design, capacity limits, better barricading and real-time monitoring. For the millions who travel to Puri, it also raises hard questions about balancing open access to a sacred ritual with basic safety.
depth.why_now: The surge happened at the peak moment of the festival, when the largest crowds gather to pull the chariots after the traditional rituals — the same high-density window that has repeatedly turned dangerous. Heavy monsoon rain and the crush of devotees at a narrow chokepoint tipped an already-packed crowd into a deadly surge.
depth.watch_next: The confirmed death toll and the findings of any official inquiry into how the crush happened despite the large police deployment.

key_terms.definition: The annual 'chariot festival' in Puri, Odisha, where huge crowds pull the deities Jagannath, Balabhadra and Subhadra through the streets on giant wooden chariots.
The main eastern entrance of Puri's Jagannath Temple, a natural chokepoint where the festival's densest crowds gather.
When a densely packed crowd surges or collapses, people can be trampled or suffocated; deaths often come from compression, not trampling — you can't breathe when the crowd squeezes too tightly.

cards.title: India's first private rocket goes for orbit: Skyroot sets July 18 for the maiden launch of Vikram-1 from Sriharikota
cards.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, Skyroot announced it will attempt to launch Vikram-1 on July 18 at 11:30 a.m. IST from the first launch pad at the Satish Dhawan Space Centre in Sriharikota. Named Mission Aagaman ('arrival'), the seven-storey, multi-stage rocket — built with an all-carbon-composite body and in-house engines, some of them 3D-printed — is designed to carry small satellites weighing up to 350 kg into low Earth orbit (roughly 450 km up). The company says all stages are already stacked on the pad and final pre-launch checks are complete.
key_points: Skyroot to launch Vikram-1 on July 18 at 11:30 a.m. from Sriharikota
Would be India's first privately built rocket to reach orbit
7-storey rocket, 3D-printed engines; carries up to 350 kg to low orbit
Built by ex-ISRO engineers — a test of India's new private space industry
cards.moot: Space is shifting from a government-only endeavour into a private industry, and the first orbital launch is the gate a private space sector must clear before it can compete. When a country lets startups build rockets, launches tend to get cheaper and more frequent — the same shift SpaceX drove in the US, now playing out in India.

depth.background: For decades, India's space programme was a government monopoly run by ISRO, the national space agency. In 2020 the government opened the sector to private companies and set up a regulator, IN-SPACe, to license them — aiming to grow a much bigger commercial space economy. Skyroot Aerospace, founded in 2018 by former ISRO engineers, became the first Indian private firm to send up a rocket in 2022, but that was a small 'sub-orbital' test — a hop that goes up and falls back down, not a full flight to orbit.
depth.why_it_matters: If Vikram-1 reaches orbit, it would be the first rocket built by a private Indian company ever to do so — clearing the single hardest technical gate in the launch business and proving India's young commercial space industry can compete. It signals India is following the path the US took with firms like SpaceX, where private rockets made launches cheaper and more frequent, and it strengthens India's bid for a bigger share of the fast-growing global market for launching small satellites.
depth.ripple_effects: A success would validate India's 2020 decision to open space to private players and could unlock more funding for Skyroot and rivals like Agnikul, while drawing satellite customers who want cheaper, on-demand rides to orbit. It would also ease ISRO's workload, letting the national agency focus on flagship missions while private firms handle routine small launches. A failure — common in maiden rocket flights — would be a setback but not unusual: even SpaceX's first three orbital attempts blew up before it succeeded.
depth.why_now: The date follows years of engine testing and Skyroot's successful 2022 sub-orbital flight; with all stages now stacked and checks done, the company locked in the July 18 window — riding a government push to build a roughly $44bn Indian space economy by 2033.
depth.watch_next: Whether the July 18 launch actually lifts off on schedule — weather and last-minute checks often force delays — and, crucially, whether Vikram-1 successfully reaches orbit.
depth.market_link: Skyroot is a privately held startup, so there's no direct listed stock to trade — but a successful orbital launch would lift sentiment across India's space-and-defence supply chain and the broader 'private space' investment theme.

key_terms.definition: A rocket powerful enough to reach the speed and height needed to put a satellite into orbit around Earth — far harder than a 'sub-orbital' hop that goes up and comes straight back down.
The band of space roughly 200–2,000 km above Earth where most small satellites — and the International Space Station — circle the planet.
India's space regulator, created in 2020 to open the sector, long an ISRO monopoly, to private companies.

cards.title: India's fiercest federal fight is back: the Modi government revives the 'delimitation' bill that could shift power from the south to the north — and Kharge demands an all-party meeting
cards.what_happened: The Modi government is preparing to bring the Constitution (131st Amendment) Bill, 2026 back before Parliament when the Monsoon session opens on July 20, with 'revised proposals' after the original was defeated on April 17 (298 votes for, 230 against — well short of the two-thirds majority a constitutional change requires). The bill would delink women's reservation from the census timeline and allow delimitation using 2011 census data, potentially expanding the Lok Sabha from 543 seats toward as many as 848. On July 16, Congress president Mallikarjun Kharge wrote to Prime Minister Modi demanding an all-party meeting to discuss the revised plan; the government has separately called an all-party meeting for July 19. The ruling NDA alliance is estimated to be around six seats short of the numbers it needs and is courting outside parties — with NCP (Sharad Pawar) leader Supriya Sule hinting her party might support the bill under certain conditions.
key_points: Govt to revive the Constitution (131st Amendment) Bill in the July 20 Monsoon session
Bill ties 33% women's reservation to redrawing Lok Sabha seats by population
Southern states fear losing seats for having controlled their populations
It lost in April; NDA is now ~6 seats short of the two-thirds it needs
cards.moot: How you count people decides who holds power. Tying representation to raw population sounds fair, but it can quietly punish regions that did what was asked of them — so in a diverse federation, 'one person, one vote' constantly collides with 'don't let the biggest states swallow the smaller ones'. That tension is the permanent balancing act of any large democracy.

depth.background: India's Lok Sabha (the directly elected lower house of Parliament) has 543 seats, each roughly meant to represent an equal number of people. But the number and boundaries of those seats — a process called 'delimitation' — have been frozen since the 1970s to avoid punishing states that slowed their population growth. Meanwhile, a 2023 law promised to reserve one-third of all seats for women, but tied that promise to a fresh delimitation based on a new census. The result is a knot: you can't fully deliver women's reservation without redrawing the seats, and redrawing the seats reopens a decades-old regional wound. In April 2026 the government's attempt to untie that knot — the Constitution (131st Amendment) Bill — was defeated on the floor of the Lok Sabha.
depth.why_it_matters: This is a fight over the most basic currency of a democracy — how many seats, and therefore how much national power, each region gets. Southern and some north-eastern states successfully slowed their population growth over decades; a headcount-based redraw would hand more seats to the faster-growing north (Uttar Pradesh, Bihar), effectively reducing the south's relative voice in Parliament. So a technical-sounding 'delimitation' bill actually decides whether the states that did what the country asked — control population — get penalised for it.
depth.ripple_effects: If it passes, the political map tilts north: projections suggest that even with an expanded house, states like Tamil Nadu and Kerala could see their share of seats shrink while Uttar Pradesh, Bihar and Rajasthan gain — reshaping which regions parties must win to rule India. It could inflame the north-south divide, energising regional parties in the south to campaign against 'northern domination', and strain Centre-state relations over funding and language. If it fails again, women's reservation stays stuck in limbo, and the government spends political capital with nothing to show. Either way, it sets the tone for the whole Monsoon session and the run-up to future elections.
depth.why_now: The Monsoon session of Parliament begins July 20, giving the government its next window to try again after April's defeat; with the numbers still tight, both sides are manoeuvring this week — the government calling an all-party meeting for July 19 and the Opposition (via Kharge's July 16 letter) demanding one of its own and vowing to stay united against the bill.
depth.watch_next: The July 19 all-party meeting and whether the government tables the revised bill on July 20 — and if it does, whether fence-sitters like the NCP (SP) give it the extra votes needed for a two-thirds majority.

key_terms.definition: The redrawing of electoral constituency boundaries and the number of seats each state gets, usually based on the latest population count.
A proposed change to India's constitution linking women's reservation to a fresh delimitation; changing the constitution needs a two-thirds majority in Parliament, not a simple one.
The idea that states which successfully slowed population growth would lose parliamentary seats under a population-based redraw — being 'punished' for good policy.
A 2023 law setting aside one-third of Lok Sabha and state-assembly seats for women, but only after a new delimitation — which is why the two issues are now tangled together.

cards.disclaimer: Decoded from public reporting for general understanding. Verify before relying on any single claim; details evolve.
